posts.name: Dale Bowers
posts.cooked:
Opened a 2018 Moreau-Naudet Montanans Chablis to quaff while watching football. Maybe it’s the ripe vintage but the the wine just lacked the acid finish I like in Chablis. Noticed a bottle of citric acid on the counter we used to can all the extra tomatoes we had.

Put a pinch in an open glass and was surprised how much lift and balance it added. Am I a heretic or has anyone else tried this?

posts.name: Mel_Hill
posts.cooked: I have not but I’m totally going to! Next time i’ve got a flabby white I’ll give it a shot.

posts.name: Jim Cowan
posts.cooked:
My experience is that citric acid is tricky during the winemaking process, Tartaric, on the other hand, is much more user friendly.
But after the fact, I’ve never tried it - whatever works - and 2018 in Chablis produced some fairly heavy wines - so good on ya.

posts.name: Mel_Hill
posts.cooked:
got that too! maybe a taste test between critic and cream
of tartar?

posts.name: Ken Lobel
posts.cooked: Cream of tartar is the salt from tartaric acid, I would think what you want is pure tartaric acid.

posts.name: Mel_Hill
posts.cooked:
KenL:

I would think what you want is pure tartaric acid

As a consumer could I just save the “wine diamonds”
found in some white wines and add those?

posts.name: Ken Lobel
posts.cooked: I’m not really sure. I was just commenting as someone who uses all three, although in very small amounts in my work in a pastry kitchen. I’ve never actually added either to wine at home but I don’t see why not.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Citric is certainly employed by some winemakers pre-bottling to add a more ‘citric’ quality to the wine.

It is not often added earlier in the process because it’s shown as a precursor for some oretty negative reactions.

Cheers

posts.name: Rohit 13h@ñ
posts.cooked: I have many times thought about adding tartaric acid powder to wine. Never done it but this makes me more inclined to try it!

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Tartaric acid is something so simple to add to wine - it’s the most prevalent acid found in grapes. It is something that many winemakers ‘refuse’ to add because they prefer to do as little as possible to go from grape to finished wine.

Adding acid to finished wine is akin to adding salt to food …

A little goes a long way to creating ‘balance’ and ‘harmony,…

Cheers

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: I add a little olive brine and a sprig of lavender into my glass of Paso Syrah so it tastes like Cote Rotie.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: I guess if you are stuck drinking bad wine it doesn’t hurt to try?

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Mel_Hill:
KenL:

I would think what you want is pure tartaric acid

As a consumer could I just save the “wine diamonds”
found in some white wines and add those?

I think they’re inert, aren’t they? That’s why you can ignore them.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Chris_Seiber:

I add a little olive brine and a sprig of lavender into my glass of Paso Syrah so it tastes like Cote Rotie.

A blueberry or two will also add a certain je ne sais pas.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked: I add some minerals to VDP Chard to make it taste like Chablis.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Richard Flack:

I add some minerals to VDP Chard to make it taste like Chablis.

You could drop some oyster shells into the decanter and let them infuse.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked: At the risk of thread drift … this reminds me of a friend of my parents who had a jar of “martini stones” infusing in vermouth; you dropped a couple into your glass of gin. They were polished marble so not much infusion going on, it was just surface tension adding a few drops of vermouth.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
John_Morris:

I think they’re inert, aren’t they? That’s why you can ignore them.

They are insoluble in the wine they dropped out of. Another wine might be able to hold tartaric acid better so they might dissolve there.

larry_schaffer:

Citric is certainly employed by some winemakers pre-bottling to add a more ‘citric’ quality to the wine.

It is not often added earlier in the process because it’s shown as a precursor for some oretty negative reactions.

This indeed. One can add citric acid to a wine only after the MLF and it is safe to assume the ML bacteria are knocked or filtered out, because ML bacteria do not convert only malic acid to lactic acid, but they also convert citric acid to acetic and pyruvic acid - and the latter gets further metabolized into diacetyl. Put a good dose of citric acid into wine before MLF and you get a nice vinegary wine with a healthy dose of buttery richness. Yummy!

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Otto_Forsberg:

Put a good dose of citric acid into wine before MLF and you get a nice vinegary wine with a healthy dose of buttery richness. Yummy!

I’m imagining the tasting note. 93 points, I’d guess.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: I like adding some critic acid into my wine at times. Maybe Parker gives the wine 86 points and calls it shrill, fruitless and catering to coastal somm elites before I try my first sip.

posts.name: Jim Cowan
posts.cooked:
Chris_Seiber:

I like adding some critic acid into my wine at times. Maybe Parker gives the wine 86 points and calls it shrill, fruitless and catering to coastal somm elites before I try my first sip.

High praise.

posts.cooked:
Otto_Forsberg:

Put a good dose of citric acid into wine before MLF and you get a nice vinegary wine with a healthy dose of buttery richness. Yummy!

LOL!

posts.name: Jon H a r m s
posts.cooked: I like to swirl around used bandaids to replicate some of that old skool brett
posts.display_username: Jon H a r m s

posts.name: Mariano Chiaramonte
posts.cooked: You can add orange juice and voilà, you have a basic sangria

posts.name: W_Shearer
posts.cooked: I have half a bottle of 1990 Sauternes that is a bit lacking in acid; adding a pinch of either citric or tartaric acid would do it some good!

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked: Just realised citric is an anagram of critic. No idea why that came to mind. Critic acid?

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked: I can see it now Dean & Deluca selling little tins of wine adjuncts…. Tartaric acid, Saddle Leather grains, Pencil Shavings, Lemon zest, Forest Floor (compost) and, yes , bandaids.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: There was one (now retired) producer in Meursault who back in the day supposedly acidified with citric rather than tartaric acid because it was cheaper… The wines are very sought-after today.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked: Gentlemen never acidify and tell?
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked: No, the wine diamonds are potassium tartrate, a salt as noted above. To improve acidity you would want tartaric acid. As a consumer you could add citric, and it could definitely improve a flabby wines. But as Larry noted, adding it during production of a wine can produce some very negative reactions in the wine. That said, I know of winemakers who have added citric and not had any issues. But it’s a risky choice.
